Project Description:
This village explores housing as a framework for community, centered on an inward-facing mews that fosters everyday social interaction. Rather than treating circulation as a purely functional element, the design repositions access as a social spine, where front doors, elevated walkways, and shared outdoor spaces collectively encourage informal encounters and cultivate belonging.

The site strategy is anchored by the retention of an existing mature tree, preserving a natural gathering point and organizes the public realm. From this anchor, the proposal establishes a deliberate gradient of public, semi-public, and private spaces, that supports both collective activity and individual retreat. The central mews serves as the primary organizing element, framed by residential units whose primary entrances face inward to enhance visibility, safety, and community engagement.

The building massing responds sensitively to context and program. A curved residential form defines the site's interior while lifting at strategic moments to introduce natural light, permeability, and vertical connections. Exterior walkways and terraces extend circulation into shared space, enabling residents to engage with the mews beyond their unit thresholds.

A diverse mix of unit types (studios, one, two, three-bedroom units) is distributed throughout the building to accommodate varied household needs. Integrated refugee housing is dispersed across multiple levels and locations to promote inclusion within the broader village rather than isolation. Proximity to shared amenities reinforces accessibility and social integration.

Precedents such as Peter Barber Architects' Edgewood Mews and Barcelona's Born Market inform the spatial and landscape strategies, particularly the use of simple forms, topography, and pedestrian-focused sequences to support flexible social use. Ultimately, Village Mews is a resilient system for fostering connection, community resilience, and shared urban life.

Home_Studio: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
The Interstice reimagines a site currently occupied by a church, proposing a redevelopment that better serves both the congregation and the surrounding community. The project introduces shared spaces designed to support community use, in collaboration with the nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS of BC).

As one of three residential buildings within a broader master plan, two of which are developed by Kyon Gicole, The Interstice focuses on providing housing for those seeking refuge. The unit mix prioritizes two- and three-bedroom layouts to accommodate families and larger households.

The building employs a single-loaded corridor strategy, enabling cross ventilation for passive cooling within the units. This exterior corridor also creates a generous overhang, offering shade during the summer while allowing solar access in the winter. Beyond its environmental performance, the corridor functions as a shared social space, encouraging interaction and fostering a sense of community among residents.

Outdoor areas are shaped by the building footprints, responding directly to site conditions and user needs. A tiered master plan minimizes shadowing between structures while maintaining adequate space for housing and essential services.

The Interstice demonstrates how thoughtful redevelopment can give back to the community, creating opportunities for connection, support, and shared public life through the spaces it defines.

Tiered building heights minimize shading and provide a gradual transition from the proposed site to the existing 3-4 storey buildings. Lastly, voids were cut in the forms of the building as a response to the existing and proposed trees on the site.

Project Description:
Designed in collaboration with Alessandro Di Bari, the site seeks to reinterpret the spatial qualities of the traditional European city square such as enclosure, walkability, and social interaction, then translating these principles into the contemporary urban fabric of Vancouver. The nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC building and the relocated church are incorporated into the perimeter of the square, functioning as its walls and creating an enclosed yet permeable space. 

The Mini-square, located on the east parcel of the site and closest to the church further enforces the concept of the European square through its symmetry, brickwork, and articulated vertical and horizontal framing. The southeastern building includes a community kitchen and rooftop balcony, providing shared spaces to support the congregation, residents, and the broader local community.

Project Description: The wall of Resilience is a residential project that is organized based on the shape of the property lines and it fosters an experience of transitioning from public community landscape and an indoor community dining space to semi private inner courtyard. The housing typology organizes the outdoor amenity space to be above ground on the second and fifth floors to provide a wider view spot for the outdoor space. The ground floor offers a two level elevated bike storage that has a capacity of 90 units of bike storage. This project tests how the landscape can become a combination of formal green fields for hosting wedding ceremonies for the church that is integrated with a public hardscape plaza for the visitors. The building is shaped to be a stepping form near the west entry to avoid shadows being cast onto the ISS immigrant society. Shading devices such as slidable wooden louvers are attached to the floor edge of the single loaded corridor to reduce the solar gain from the south during the summer season. The single loaded corridor has guardrails that allows residents to grow their own plantings on which creates a green south and west facade.

Project Title: Thresholds of Resilience: Sheltering the Vulnerable, Without Isolation
Image Description/Alt Text\n: A rendering of the courtyard
Home_Studio: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Option 9, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
How do we house people who have lost their homes without cutting them off from the life of a community? This question sits at the heart of Thresholds of Resilience. Responding to the "ReZoned" challenge, the project transforms underused church land into a place where below-market housing and refugee housing are not "added on" but woven in.

The masterplan starts by strengthening the existing neighborhood. A carefully renovated church is joined by a new residential block. By removing private parking, the site becomes a pedestrian-first "Shared Ground" - not a plaza in name only, but a daily gathering space with a community kitchen where church members, ISSofBC staff, and all residents can cook, eat, and linger together. This is not an event space; it is an everyday place. At street level, this thick wall opens into an inhabited colonnade - a covered walkway where neighbors can pause, meet, or simply sit without feeling exposed to the street's noise. 

The courtyard designed for calm, not spectacle. Gentle water features mask city sounds. A canopy of pink cherry blossoms - chosen to honor the magnolia's quiet symbolism of resilience and home-offers shade and beauty. The courtyard is shared by all residents, safety here comes from mutual presence, not surveillance.

This thinking continues inside. Single-loaded corridors are facing the courtyard, act like elevated green walkway. These are places to nod hello, to see children playing below, to feel part of something. A simple structural grid allows for diverse 1-to-4-bedroom homes, with refugee housing distributed across all floors, indistinguishable from market units. Equity is not announced; it is built. Instead of exposed balconies, winter gardens are carved into the façade, private, sheltered, but still connected to the life of the building.

In the end, this project is about housing as a foundation for everyday community where people can finally rest and once again, be resilient and hopeful.

Home_Studio: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design
Project Description:
This project is part of the larger Cascade Residence development, organized as a linear housing bar that frames a continuous shared outdoor space. The design transforms a typical residential strip into a social spine, where circulation, landscape, and community life intersect.

Ground-level pathways and green spaces encourage interaction and movement, while the articulated massing breaks down the scale and creates rhythm along the length of the building. Units are arranged to maximize light, views, and connection to the shared space.

The project explores how linear living can support a more connected and community-driven residential experience.
Image 1 Alt/Description: The north façade of Courtyard Spine presents a rhythmic composition of mid-rise residential blocks organized along a continuous elevation. Variations in height, material tone, and window placement articulate each segment while maintaining an overall cohesive identity. Alternating light and dark cladding emphasizes the modular nature of the design, while recessed and projecting volumes create depth and shadow across the façade. A central break in the massing suggests a key പ്രവേശ or shared circulation point, reinforcing the idea of a connected spine that links the development together.
Image 1 Caption: Courtyard Spine – North Façade: A layered composition of modular residential blocks unified through rhythm and material contrast.
Image 2 Alt/Description: This site plan illustrates the internal courtyard of Courtyard Spine, organized as a linear landscaped sequence running through the center of the development. The space is defined by the surrounding building mass, which steps in and out to create a series of pockets and expansions along the courtyard edge. A meandering pedestrian pathway connects key nodes, including seating areas, planted zones, and small gathering spaces. A central feature—likely a plaza or focal installation—anchors the composition, while softer landscaping elements such as trees and shrubs create a more intimate, human-scaled environment. The layout balances movement and pause, encouraging both circulation and social interaction within a semi-enclosed green spine.
Image 3 Alt/Description: The south façade of Courtyard Spine presents a unified, light-clad surface articulated through stepped massing and a scattered arrangement of window openings. This seemingly irregular pattern is intentional—it translates the interior courtyard language onto the exterior. The distribution of windows reflects the beginning of the meandering path found within the landscaped courtyard, acting as an architectural expression of movement across the building’s surface. A recessed central entrance anchors the elevation, while the façade as a whole becomes a subtle diagram of the spatial experience unfolding behind it.
Image 4 Alt/Description: An east–west section of Courtyard Spine showing stepped residential units organized around a central courtyard, with integrated terraces and vertical circulation cores.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Floor plan of level 1 showing community space in the center highlighted in orange and 2-4 bed units surrounding it bike room, garbage room and mechanical room near the east entrance

Project Description: This project explores the design of a contemporary building positioned at a prominent, high-traffic intersection, responding to both urban intensity and human-scale experience. The proposal aims to create a visually engaging yet context-sensitive structure that enhances the streetscape while maintaining a sense of openness and permeability. Through careful massing, material selection, and façade articulation, the building reduces its perceived bulk and integrates harmoniously with its surroundings. The design prioritizes accessibility, functionality, and sustainability, offering a dynamic space that serves both its users and the broader community while contributing positively to the urban fabric.
